sanskrit_devanagari:
अवाच्यवादांश्च बहून्वदिष्यन्ति तवाहिताः ।
निन्दन्तस्तव सामर्थ्यं ततो दुःखतरं नु किम् ॥ ३६ ॥
sanskrit_iast:
avācya-vādāṁś ca bahūn vadiṣyanti tavāhitāḥ |
nindantas tava sāmarthyaṁ tato duḥkhataraṁ nu kim || 36 ||

sanskrit_sources.agreement: exact (raw HTML scrape; akṣara sequence identical; line-break inserted between hemistichs in source pack; vedabase renders second hemistich's 'sāmarthyam' as 'सामर्थ्य' without final anusvara — a known rendering variant; the holy-bhagavad-gita.org capture shows 'सामर्थ्यं' with anusvara, which we adopt as the canonical form per gitasupersite and bhagavad-gita.us)
exact (text body identical; uses ': ' ASCII colon for visarga in display layer; underlying akṣara sequence identical)

exact (IAST text body identical; word-segmentation differs cosmetically — 'avācya-vādāṁś' rendered as 'avacya-vadams' without IAST diacritics in this source's plain transliteration; underlying akṣara sequence identical)
exact (academic critical edition; Bhg_02.036 = MBh_06,024.036). Body text identical.

translations.verbatim_quote: Your enemies will describe you in many unkind words and scorn your ability. What could be more painful for you?
Your enemies will defame and humiliate you with unkind words, disparaging your might. Alas, what could be more painful than that?

commentaries.verbatim_excerpt: Lord Kṛṣṇa was at first surprised at Arjuna's uncalled-for plea for compassion, and He described his compassion as befitting the non-Āryans. Now in so many words, He has proved His statements against Arjuna's so-called compassion.

commentaries.translator: Swami Gambhirananda (Advaita Ashrama edition of Sankara-bhasya)
commentaries.verbatim_excerpt: 2.36 Ca, and besides; tava, your; ahitah, enemies; vadisyanti, will speak; bahun, many, various kinds of; avacya-vadan, indecent words, unutterable words; nindantah, while denigrating, scorning; tava, your; samarthyam, might earned from battles against Nivatakavaca and others. Therefore, kim nu, what can be; duhkhataram, more painful; tatah, than that, than the sorrow arising from being scorned? That is to say, there is no greater pain than it.
commentaries.summary_paraphrase: Shankara reads 2.36 as the intensification of the disgrace argument begun in 2.34-2.35. The point is the specific kind of pain: not the abstract pain of dishonor but the concrete pain of one's enemies — those who already wish you ill — speaking many indecent words while denigrating the very capability you have earned through prior battles (sāmarthyam earned against Nivātakavaca and others). The 'nu kim' rhetorical question closes the argument: there is no greater pain than this.

commentaries.translator: Swami Adidevananda (Sri Ramakrishna Math edition of Ramanuja's Gita-bhasya)
commentaries.verbatim_excerpt: 2.36 Moreover, your enemies, the sons of Dhrtarastra, will make many remarks unutterably slanderous and disparaging to heroes, saying, 'How can this Partha stand in the presence of us, who are heroes, even for a moment? His prowess is elsewhere than in our presence.' Can there be anything more painful to you than this?
commentaries.summary_paraphrase: Ramanuja makes the disparagement specific by quoting the kind of words the Kauravas would say: 'How can Pārtha stand in our presence even for a moment? His prowess shows up elsewhere, not in front of us.' The pain is not generic dishonor; it is the specific pain of one's earned prowess being publicly recharacterised as cowardice in the only forum where the recharacterisation will land. The verse closes by linking forward to 2.37: death in battle is preferable to subjection to disparagement of this kind.

commentaries.verbatim_excerpt: Not only would Arjunas estimation wane in the assembly of mighty warriors but here Lord Krishna uses the word nindantah meaning to vilify. Arjunas enemies such as Duryodhana would say many unbecoming things about him such as: Look at the impotent Arjuna fleeing the battlefield like a dog with its tail between its legs.
commentaries.summary_paraphrase: Keshava Kashmiri specifically distinguishes 'nindantaḥ' (vilification, active disparagement) from mere loss of estimation: the verse's force is that the enemies will not just think less of Arjuna but actively recharacterise his identity in a public register he cannot rebut from the sidelines.

positions.rendering: indecent words, unutterable words (avacya — that which should not be uttered)
remarks unutterably slanderous and disparaging to heroes — i.e., the specific kind of slander a warrior cannot publicly answer

disagreements_among_translators.explanation: The Sanskrit 'avācya' is technically 'that which should not be spoken' — words that breach the bounds of decent address. Prabhupada and Mukundananda flatten this to 'unkind' / 'defamatory.' Shankara preserves the technical sense ('unutterable / indecent'). Ramanuja localizes it: not just any slander, but the slander a hero specifically cannot rebut — public recharacterisation of one's prowess as cowardice in front of the heroes whose opinion was the original constituency for that prowess. The distinction matters: the verse is not about general criticism but about the specific pain of having one's professional reputation rewritten in the only forum where the rewrite has weight.

literal_meaning: Your enemies will speak many unutterable words while disparaging your prowess. What is more painful than that?
traditional_meaning_consensus: The third and most pointed verse in Krishna's three-verse sequence (2.34, 2.35, 2.36) closing the disgrace argument before the symmetric-outcome pivot at 2.37. 2.34 establishes that ill-fame outlasts death; 2.35 establishes that those whose esteem you held will reread you as cowardly; 2.36 sharpens both: it is your enemies — those who already wish you ill — who will rewrite your earned prowess (sāmarthyam) as inability, in many indecent words, in the very forum (the warrior assembly) where the rewriting has weight. The commentarial tradition (Shankara, Ramanuja, Sridhara, Keshava, Vishvanatha) reads the closing 'tato duḥkhataraṁ nu kim' as the rhetorical climax of the disgrace argument: there is no greater pain than this. The verse does not argue this is the deepest possible suffering metaphysically; it argues this is the specific suffering Arjuna's chosen retreat would produce, and it is sharper than the suffering he is trying to avoid by retreating. After 2.36, Krishna pivots in 2.37 to the symmetric outcome (death wins heaven, victory wins the earth) — the disgrace argument has done its closing work and Krishna can now offer the resolution.
